revision.text:
[[image:Samukawa Shrine 1.jpg|200px|right|thumbnail|寒川神社の本殿]] [[image:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|200px|right|thumbnail|寒川神社の神門]]
''' the Samukawa Shinto shrine''' ()It is 神社 enshrined to 神奈川県高座郡寒川町 Imperial prince mountain. It is 相模国一ﾉ宮. ..old.. 社格 is a company in country. Ancient times and the Sagamigawa measuring up are presumed that powerful some powerful clans built from going down of the Sagami country make and belonging to the platform county that is the ground of the those who introduce company Koguryo immigration.
It enshrines it on a low plateau in the left bank that went back from the mouth of a river of 相模川 by about seven kilos. It is thought by ancient times that 相模湾 enters around here, and there was Sagami 国府 in the vicinity of 海老名市 Kokubu from the Shinto shrine to 8 kilo further upstream. Respecting the court was also thick as the great shrine of 延喜式 Sagami name of a country god. Those who visit a shrine gather all around 関東 all over as a patron saint of taking off now.
About Matscami, the Samukawa wolf and the respondent god emperor, etc. sell and are being assumed to be married couple god compared with Samukawa Hifl and Samukawa now though are various opinions. There is a company Samukawa Shinto shrine in the expression also in 下総国. There are "Takeyumimats" (the eighth in the New Year), "Nationalist government festival" (May 5), "Hamaf old fashioned festival" (July 15), "浜降祭" (the third Monday in July), and "Festival" (September 20) in the festival.
Recently, ねぶた came to be decorated between until Setsubun from the new year to February by the sexagenary cycle at the god gate. When the night comes, the light is improved.

Ancient times and the Sagamigawa measuring up are presumed that powerful some powerful clans built from going down of the Sagami country make and belonging to the platform county that is the ground of the those who introduce company Koguryo immigration.
It enshrines it on a low plateau in the left bank that went back from the mouth of a river of 相模川 by about seven kilos. It is thought by ancient times that 相模湾 enters around here, and there was Sagami 国府 in the vicinity of 海老名市 Kokubu from the Shinto shrine to 8 kilo further upstream. Respecting the court was also thick as the great shrine of 延喜式 Sagami name of a country god. Those who visit a shrine gather all around 関東 all over as a patron saint of taking off now.
About Matscami, the Samukawa wolf and the respondent god emperor, etc. sell and are being assumed to be married couple god compared with Samukawa Hifl and Samukawa now though are various opinions. There is a company Samukawa Shinto shrine in the expression also in 下総国. There are "Takeyumimats" (the eighth in the New Year), "Nationalist government festival" (May 5), "Hamaf old fashioned festival" (July 15), "浜降祭" (the third Monday in July), and "Festival" (September 20) in the festival.

revision.comment: replaced machine-translated mess with infobox and basic article
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Samukawa Jinja
寒川神社 | image = Samukawa Shrine 1.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine | type = | dedication = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded = unknown | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | phone = | website = http://www.samukawa-jinja.or.jp/index02.htm }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Jinja'''|寒川神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]] in [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]]. Plutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. Page 173 ==Enshrined ''kami''== The main kami of Samukawa Jinja is the Samukawa Daimyojin, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|'''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto'''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|'''Samukawa-hime no mikoto'''|寒川比女命|}}; both apparently local ''kami'', although in the pre-[[Meiji period]], worship of [[Hachiman]] and [[Inada-hime]] appeared to be prevalent. ==History== The origins of Samukawa Jinja are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that it was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479); the earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. It was listed as the ''ichinomiya'' of Sagami Province in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, and the administration center of Sagami Province was located nearby. The shrine is located on the bank of the [[Sagami River]], about eight kilometers from the river’s mouth. [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|250px|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) ISBN 1873410638 ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Samukawa Jinja
寒川神社 | image = Samukawa Shrine 1.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine | type = | dedication = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded = unknown | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | phone = | website = http://www.samukawa-jinja.or.jp/index02.htm }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Jinja'''|寒川神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]] in [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is located on the bank of the [[Sagami River]], about eight kilometers from the river’s mouth. ==History== The origins of Samukawa Jinja are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that it was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479). The main kami of Samukawa Jinja is the Samukawa Daimyojin, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}}. Both were local ''kami''. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. Sumakawa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Sagami province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, Sumakawa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the mid-range of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|ranked, nationally significant shrines]]. ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]]. Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. ISBN-10 1873410638/13-ISBN 9781873410639; [http://www.worldcat.org/title/matsuri-the-festivals-of-japan/oclc/36221577&referer=brief_results OCLC 36221577] {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, Sumakawa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the mid-range of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|ranked, nationally significant shrines]]. ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]]. Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. ISBN 1873410638/13-ISBN 9781873410639; [http://www.worldcat.org/title/matsuri-the-festivals-of-japan/oclc/36221577&referer=brief_results OCLC 36221577] {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]]

Both were local ''kami''. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, Sumakawa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the mid-range of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|ranked, nationally significant shrines]]. ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine | type = | dedication = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded = unknown | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | address = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | phone = | website = http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]] in [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is located on the bank of the [[Sagami River]], about eight kilometers from the river’s mouth. ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that it was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479). The main kami of Samukawa Shrine is the Samukawa Daimyojin, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}}. Both were local ''kami''. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. Samukawa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Sagami province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, Sumakawa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the mid-range of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|ranked, nationally significant shrines]]. ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|accessdate=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|accessdate=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{URL|http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]] in [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that it was founded during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479). The main kami of Samukawa Shrine is the Samukawa Daimyojin, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}}. Both were local ''kami''. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. Samukawa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Sagami province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, Sumakawa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the mid-range of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|ranked, nationally significant shrines]]. ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]]

revision.comment: copyedit
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|Ominakami no mikoto|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''Kokuhei Chūsha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the[[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|
Gate
File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|

 ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

[[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|Ominakami no mikoto|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''Kokuhei Chūsha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|
Gate
File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|
[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival
File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|
Precincts
File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|
Ichi-no-Torii
File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|
Ni-no-Torii
 ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. 寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo| ''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|Ominakami no mikoto|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''Kokuhei Chūsha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-cho, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''Kokuhei Chūsha''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]]

revision.comment: v2.05b - Fix errors for [[WP:WCW|CW project]] (Category before last heading)
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]]

London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]]

The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia30]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia30]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]]

None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:dd]]

[[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Hh]]
