[[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Province]]. The main festival of the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is one of the most famous ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[s around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==En]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[d '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[''== The ''[[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]'' en]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[d at Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyō]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [['''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ are unknown. Unverifiable ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is styled as the only ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ in Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Province to be a {{nihongo|'']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]'' en]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[d. The current '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyō]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[, a sub-]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ of the [[Ise Grand ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Omina]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ ''[[Kuni]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ River passed the east side of Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[. In the ''[[Azuma Ka]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ on the birth of [[Minamoto]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[gen]] visited the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ was designated an {{nihongo|National ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[to ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]] [[Category:kkk]] [[s]]. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). Unverifiable ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is styled as the only ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ in Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Province to be a {{nihongo|'']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]'' en]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[d. The current '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyō]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[, a sub-]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ of the [[Ise Grand ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Omina]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these '']][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [['' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ ''[[Kuni]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ River passed the east side of Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[. In the ''[[Azuma Ka]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sa]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ on the birth of [[Minamoto]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[gen]] visited the ]][[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects to break]]__NOINDEX__[[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]] [[ and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Hh]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Hh]] [[Category:not stubs]]

The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hh]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:not stubs]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hh]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:not stubs]]

It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi]][[category:no breaking]][[category:Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni]][[category:no breaking]][[category:Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi]][[category:no breaking]][[category:Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni]][[category:no breaking]][[category:Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:not stubs]] [[Category:broken nos]]

[[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Samukawa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:not stubs]] [[Category:broken nos]]

The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] 寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] 寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|''[[kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] 寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[Miyayama Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[Nebuta]] in [[Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G.
