{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

(1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q48744]] Wikidata name: Samukawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|22|43|N|139|22|53|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]]

[[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]]}} [[sn:Samukawa Shrine]] [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[sn:amukawa Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}}

By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Samukawa Shrine]][[de:Samukawa-Schrein]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[fr:Samukawa-jinja]][[ja:寒川神社]][[zh:寒川神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48744]] Wikidata name: Samukawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = File:The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.37956667|N|139.38335278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Samukawa|WD=Q1342787}} }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}}

Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. [[sn:commons:Category:Samukawa Shrine]][[de:Samukawa-Schrein]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[fr:Samukawa-jinja]][[ja:寒川神社]][[zh:寒川神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48744]] Wikidata name: Samukawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = File:The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.37956667|N|139.38335278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Samukawa|WD=Q1342787}} }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Samukawa Shrine]][[de:Samukawa-Schrein]][[en:Samukawa Shrine]][[fr:Samukawa-jinja]][[ja:寒川神社]][[zh:寒川神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q48744]] Wikidata name: Samukawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Samukawa Shrine
寒川神社 | image = File:The Samukawa Shrine 02.jpg | caption = Honden of Samukawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kanagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.37956667|N|139.38335278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} | type = | deity = Samukawa Daimyojin | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 3916 Miyayama, Samukawa-machi, Koza-gun, Kanagawa | website = {{Official|1=http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = | festival = September 20 | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Samukawa|WD=Q1342787}} }} {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Shrine'''|寒川神社|Samukawa jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Miyayama neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Samukawa, Kanagawa|Samukawa]], [[:en:Kōza District, Kanagawa|Kōza District]]. [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} This shrine is one of the most famous shrines around [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], where about 2 million people visit each year. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Samukawa Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Samukawa Daimyōjin'''| 寒川大明神|}}, an amalgamation of the male {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hiko no mikoto''|寒川比古命|}} and the female {{nihongo|''Samukawa-hime no mikoto''|寒川比女命|}} ==History== The origins of Samukawa Shrine are unknown. Unverifiable shrine legend states that during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] (418-479), messengers were sent to this shrine from the imperial court. The earliest written records indicate that the shrine was rebuilt in the year 727, and its name also appears in the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' entry for the year 846. By the time of the 923 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is styled as the only shrine in Sagami Province to be a {{nihongo|''Myōjin-taisha''|名神大社||}}. There is also a mystery regarding the ''[[kami]]'' enshrined. The current ''kami'' are given the collective name of "Samukawa Daimyōjin", about whom nothing is known. One theory identifies these ''kami'' as being associated with [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], as they are also worshipped at the Muyano Shrine, a sub-shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Another theory identifies them as the children of {{nihongo|''Ominakami no mikoto''|大水上命|}}, who may or may to be the same as [[Ōyamatsumi]]. It is also possible that these ''kami'' are the ancestors of the Samukawa clan, the Sagami ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]''. None of these theories are mutually exclusive.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} At present, the shrine is located on a low plateau on the left bank of the [[:en:Sagami River|Sagami River]], about seven kilometers inland in the central southern part of Kanagawa Prefecture; however, during the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], it was located on the shore of an inlet of [[:en:Sagami Bay|Sagami Bay]] which extended far inland from the present shoreline. The ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway crossed the Sagami River passed the east side of Samukawa Shrine. In the ''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Samukawa Shrine is identified as the [[ichinomiya]] of Sagami Province, and there was a dedication ceremony at the shrine on the birth of [[Minamoto no Yoriie]]. Afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], and in the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]]. [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] visited the shrine and donated a ''[[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'' helmet and sword to pray for victory when he attacked [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]] in October 1569. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], in 1871 the shrine was designated an {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|''[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]''}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

The shrine is a five-minute walk from [[:en:Miyayama Station|Miyayama Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sagami Line|Sagami Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:寒川神社 神門.JPG|{{center|Gate}} File:Samukawa Shrine-Shinmon lightup.JPG|{{center|[[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]]-style decorations on gate during festival}} File:Samukawa Shrine gateway.jpg|{{center|Precincts}} File:寒川神社 一の鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ichi-no-Torii}} File:寒川神社 大鳥居.JPG|{{center|Ni-no-Torii}} ==Festivals== [[File:Samukawa Shrine 2.jpg|right|thumb|Main gate of Samukawa Shrine]] The main festival of the shrine is held annually on September 20, and features ''[[yabusame]]'' performances. During the [[Setsubun]] festival in February, illuminated paper figures are hung from the main gate in a style similar to that of the [[:en:Nebuta|Nebuta]] in [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]].Plutschow. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan,'' p. 173. ==Related information== [[:en:Brooklyn Botanic Garden|Brooklyn Botanic Garden]] in [[:en:New York City|New York City]] has a ''[[Mikoshi]]'' donated by Samukawa Shrine.{{Cite web|title=Brooklyn Botanic Garden Official Website|url=https://www.bbg.org/news/a_mikoshi_a_portable_shinto_shrine|access-date=2018-08-19}} ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbert and P.G. O'Neil. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: Routledge. {{ISBN|9781873410639}}; {{OCLC|36221577}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://samukawajinjya.jp/en/ Samukawa-jinjya Shrine Official Website]{{in lang|en}} *{{instagram|samukawa_jinja_official|寒川神社/Samukawa Jinja Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kanagawa Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Samukawa Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Samukawa Shrine}} [[de:Samukawa-Schrein]] [[fr:Samukawa-jinja]] [[ja:寒川神社]] [[zh:寒川神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48744}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
